coredata.dc:title: Nanosheet-structured NiCoO2/carbon nanotubes hybrid composite as a novel bifunctional oxygen electrocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Li
Zhou, Han
Sun, Yao
Xin, Shuli
Xiao, Chunhui
Kumatani, Akichika
Matsue, Tomokazu
Zhang, Penghui
Ding, Shujiang
Li, Fei
coredata.dc:description: Synthesis of novel bifunctional catalysts based on earth-abundant elements with high catalytic activities for both oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) becomes a main strategy to promote the development of metal-air batteries and fuel cells. Herein, a novel Co2+-based mixed transition metal oxide composite, the three-dimensional (3D) nanosheet-structured NiCoO2/carbon nanotubes (CNTs) composite, was successfully synthesized through a facile one-pot method. The better electrocatalytic activities of NiCoO2/CNTs for both ORR and OER than those of pure NiCoO2, CNTs, physically mixed NiCoO2 and CNTs, and Co3O4/CNTs are proved. The comparison of local ORR and OER electrocatalytic activities of NiCoO2/CNTs to other catalysts are obtained by scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM) characterizations. The closed oxygen reduction peak and half-wave potentials, nearly four-electron dominated process, better methanol tolerance and electrochemical stability confirm the comparable electrocatalytic performance and better stability of NiCoO2/CNTs to the commercial Pt/C catalyst for ORR. The similar stability of NiCoO2/CNTs demonstrates its comparable electrocatalytic performance to the state-of-the-art RuO2 catalyst for OER. The as-prepared NiCoO2/CNTs composite presents as a promising bifunctional oxygen electrocatalyst in alkaline solution for potential applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NiCoO2/carbon nanotubes composite
Nanosheet structure
Oxygen reduction reaction
Oxygen evolution reaction
Scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure, composition and electric resistance of the catalysts
Electrocatalytic activities of the catalysts for ORR
Electrocatalytic activities of the catalysts for OER
Bifunctional oxygen electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs
Methanol tolerance and electrochemical stability of NiCoO2/CNTs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21105079 , 51273158 , 21405119 ), the Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars by the State Education Ministry of China , the International Science and Technology Cooperation and Exchange Program of Shaanxi Province of China ( 2016KW-064 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China , the General Financial Grant from the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2016M592773 ), the Chinese Government Scholarship ( 201506285022 ) and the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (KAKENHI: 16H06042 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of the NiCoO2/CNTs composite.
SEM images of (a) NiCoO2/CNTs and (c) Co3O4/CNTs. TEM images of (b) NiCoO2/CNTs and (d) Co3O4/CNTs. XRD spectra of (e) NiCoO2/CNTs and (f) Co3O4/CNTs.

(a) CV curves of different samples modified GCE in O2-saturated (solid curves) or N2-saturared (dashed curves) 0.1M KOH aqueous solution at scan rates of 10mVs−1. From top to bottom, the bare GCE, the GCE modified with NiCoO2, NiCoO2 +CNTs, CNTs, Co3O4/CNTs, NiCoO2/CNTs and Pt/C, respectively. (b) LSV curves of the glassy-carbon RDE modified with different samples in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution at a scan rate of 10mVs−1 and the rotating speed of 1600rpm. From top to bottom, the RDE modified with NiCoO2, CNTs, NiCoO2 +CNTs, Co3O4/CNTs, NiCoO2/CNTs and Pt/C, respectively. (c) LSV curves of the glassy-carbon RDE modified with NiCoO2/CNTs at a scan rate of 10mVs−1 and various rotating speeds; (d) K–L plots of the NiCoO2/CNTs composite under different applied potentials and at different rotating speeds.

(a) Optical photograph of the catalyst spots of NiCoO2/CNTs (upper-left), Co3O4/CNTs (upper-right), NiCoO2 +CNTs (bottom-left) and NiCoO2 (bottom-right) loaded on a GCE. (b-f) Background current-subtracted SECM area scan images of the sample spots in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH aqueous solution with different potentials applied at GCE (E S): (b) 0.56, (c) 0.61, (d) 0.66, (e) 0.71 and (f) 0.76V (vs. RHE). SECM tip diameter: 25μm, tip-to-catalyst distances: ca. 50μm, tip potential: 0.16V (vs. RHE). (g) Background current-subtracted SECM x-line scans (at y =400μm) along the most active sites of NiCoO2/CNTs and Co3O4/CNTs spots at different catalyst potentials and (i) the corresponding bar graph. (h) Background current-subtracted SECM y-line scans (at x =420μm) along the most active areas of NiCoO2/CNTs and NiCoO2 +CNTs spots at different catalyst potentials and (j) the corresponding bar diagram.

(a) LSV curves of different samples modified GCE in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH aqueous solution at scan rates of 10mVs−1. From top to bottom, the GCE modified with RuO2, NiCoO2/CNTs, NiCoO2 +CNTs, Co3O4/CNTs, Pt/C, NiCoO2 and CNTs, respectively. Inset: the magnified view of the LSV curves of NiCoO2/CNTs, Co3O4/CNTs and pure NiCoO2 in the potential range of 0.95 to 1.55V. (b) The corresponding Tafel plots (a, RuO2; b, NiCoO2/CNTs; c, NiCoO2 +CNTs; d, Co3O4/CNTs; e, Pt/C; f, NiCoO2; g, CNTs).

(a) Optical photograph of the catalyst spots of NiCoO2/CNTs (upper-left), Co3O4/CNTs (upper-right), NiCoO2 +CNTs (bottom-left) and NiCoO2 (bottom-right) loaded on a GCE. (b-d) Background current-subtracted SECM area scan images of the sample spots in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH aqueous solution with different potentials applied at GCE (E S): (b) 1.51, (c) 1.56, (d) 1.61V (vs. RHE). SECM tip diameter: 25μm, tip-to-catalyst distances: ca. 50μm, tip potential: 0.16V (vs. RHE). (e) Background current-subtracted SECM x-line scans (at y =550μm) along the most active sites of NiCoO2/CNTs and Co3O4/CNTs spots at different catalyst potentials and (g) the corresponding bar graph. (f) Background current-subtracted SECM y-line scans (at x =500μm) along the most active areas of NiCoO2/CNTs and NiCoO2 +CNTs spots at different catalyst potentials and (h) the corresponding bar diagram.

CV curves of (a) NiCoO2/CNTs and (b) the commercial Pt/C modified GCE in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH before and after adding 3M methanol. Chronoamperometric curves of NiCoO2/CNTs and Pt/C on GCE in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH at 0.76V (vs. RHE) (c) before and after adding 3M methanol at around 300s and (d) for 10000s. (e) Chronopotentiometric curves of NiCoO2/CNTs, Pt/C and RuO2 in the O2-saturated 0.1M KOH electrolyte at a constant current density of 10mAcm‐2 with a rotation speed of 900rpm.

head.ce:title: Nanosheet-structured NiCoO2/carbon nanotubes hybrid composite as a novel bifunctional oxygen electrocatalyst

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of novel bifunctional catalysts based on earth-abundant elements with high catalytic activities for both oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) becomes a main strategy to promote the development of metal-air batteries and fuel cells. Herein, a novel Co2+-based mixed transition metal oxide composite, the three-dimensional (3D) nanosheet-structured NiCoO2/carbon nanotubes (CNTs) composite, was successfully synthesized through a facile one-pot method. The better electrocatalytic activities of NiCoO2/CNTs for both ORR and OER than those of pure NiCoO2, CNTs, physically mixed NiCoO2 and CNTs, and Co3O4/CNTs are proved. The comparison of local ORR and OER electrocatalytic activities of NiCoO2/CNTs to other catalysts are obtained by scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM) characterizations. The closed oxygen reduction peak and half-wave potentials, nearly four-electron dominated process, better methanol tolerance and electrochemical stability confirm the comparable electrocatalytic performance and better stability of NiCoO2/CNTs to the commercial Pt/C catalyst for ORR. The similar stability of NiCoO2/CNTs demonstrates its comparable electrocatalytic performance to the state-of-the-art RuO2 catalyst for OER. The as-prepared NiCoO2/CNTs composite presents as a promising bifunctional oxygen electrocatalyst in alkaline solution for potential applications.

ce:section.ce:para: To fulfill the increasing demands for clean and sustainable energy, researchers have devoted much effort to developing cost-effective, eco-friendly new energy storage and conversion systems, such as metal-air batteries, fuel cells and water splitting devices. In these sustainable energy technologies, oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) are the decisive key electrode processes [1–6]. However, the inherently sluggish kinetics of oxygen reduction and evolution processes, which involve multi-electron transfer steps, significantly influence the efficiency of these energy conversion and storage technologies, thus limiting their developments and practical applications. Platinum (i.e., Pt/carbon (Pt/C)) and its alloys [7–9], and iridium and ruthenium composites (i.e., IrO2 and RuO2) [10–13] are the state-of-the-art ORR and OER catalysts, respectively, which have the disadvantages of prohibitive cost, scarcity and poor electrochemical stability. Therefore, design and synthesis of the bifunctional electrocatalysts based on earth-abundant elements and capable of driving both ORR and OER becomes the main strategy to promote the development of sustainable new energy devices.

Cobalt-based transition metal oxides (Co-TMOs) (e.g., Co3O4) are one of the promising bifunctional oxygen electrocatalysts owning to the exposed sites of Co3+ ions for chemisorption of O2 in their spinel-structure and the merits of environmental friendliness, low cost and good electrochemical stability [1–6,14–16]. In recent years, incorporations of other eco-friendly cheap element atoms (e.g., Ni, Fe, Mn) into Co3O4 to form the Co-based mixed transition metal oxides (Co-MTMOs) (e.g., NiCo2O4 [4,5], CoFe2O4 [2,17,18] and MnCo2O4 [19]) exhibit even higher bifunctional oxygen electrocatalytic activity than that of Co3O4. It can be contributed to the synergetic effect between Co3+ and the second element atom, and their relatively low activation energy [20,21]. In various Co-MTMOs composites, nanostructured NiCoxOy composites have demonstrated high electrocatalytic performances for both ORR and OER, which could own to their nanostructured structure with high specific surface area for adsorbing oxygen and the lower activation energy with incorporation of Ni2+ cation [4,5,20,21]. Through synthesizing NiCoxOy composites on highly conductive nanocarbon materials (e.g., graphene [5,22]), the electron transfer process of ORR/OER can be further facilitated, and the introduced carbon substrate can also prevent the aggregation of nanostructured NiCoxOy composites and provide new active sites for catalytic reaction [23]. But most of these studies are focusing on the Co3+-based MTMOs composites as ORR catalysts. Recently, the surface Co2+ ions of Co3O4 have also been reported to be catalytically active for ORR, which could be due to that oxygen molecules are more preferably adsorbed on the catalytically active sites of Co2+ (3d54s2) cations via the Pauling mode rather than the surface Co3+ (3d54s1) cations [24]. The interaction of the Co2+-rich surface into composites has been proven to be more beneficial to ORR catalysis [25–28], e.g., the CoO/nitrogen-doped CNT composite [25] and the CoO nanoparticles/carbon support composite [26]. Therefore, considering the active sites of surface Co2+ for ORR and the synergetic effect between Ni2+ and Co2+, the Ni-Co2+-based MTMOs could also be a good ORR and also bifunctional oxygen catalyst candidate. However, there is no report on developing Ni-Co2+ oxide composites as ORR or bifunctional oxygen catalyst yet.

In this report, we synthesized a novel Co2+-based MTMOs hybrid, the three-dimensional (3D) nanosheet-structured NiCoO2/CNTs composite through growing NiCoO2 on CNTs using a facile one-pot method. A Co3O4/CNTs composite was also synthesized as the Co-TMOs reference for comparison. The electrocatalytic activities, the reaction pathway and kinetic information of the as-prepared NiCoO2/CNTs composite for both ORR and OER in alkaline solution were studied using cyclic voltammetry (CV), rotating disk electrode (RDE) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) techniques. Scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM), with the capabilities of characterizing local reactivity of catalyst with high spatial resolution, rapidly evaluating electrocatalytic activities of several catalysts in one experiment, and in situ “visualizing” the catalytic process occurred on the sample surface [29–39], was also employed to characterize and compare the local ORR and OER electrocatalytic activities of the catalyst samples. The electrochemical stability and the methanol tolerance of the NiCoO2/CNTs composite for ORR and OER were finally evaluated by chronoamperometric and chronopotentiometric measurements.

The NiCoO2/CNTs composite was synthesized through a one-pot method [40,41], as illustrated in Fig. 1 . 15mg CNTs modified with sulfonated polystyrene layer (CNTs-SPS) prepared according to our previous report [40] was dispersed in 40mL deionized water and followed by addition of Ni(NO)3 ·6H2O (75mg), Co(NO3)2 ·6H2O (75mg), hexamethylenetetramine (35.5mg) and citric acid trisodium salt (7.25mg), respectively. After dissolution, the solution was slowly stirred and heated at 90°C for 6hours, and centrifuged at 7000rpm for 3min. Then, the accumulation in the solution was washed thoroughly by ethanol and then dried at 60°C overnight. The as-prepared NiCo-precursor/CNTs was calcined at 450°C for 2hours with a heating rate of 1°Cmin−1 under nitrogen and the NiCoO2/CNTs composite product was finally obtained. The synthetic procedure of the Co3O4/CNTs composite was similar to that of the NiCoO2/CNTs, except without adding Ni(NO)3 ·6H2O in the reactant mixture.

The morphology of the as-prepared catalyst samples was characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JEOL JSM-7000F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100). The crystallographic information and the chemical states of the samples were characterized by a powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu, Lab X XRD-6000) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos, Axis Ultra) at monochromatic A1 K-αradiation (150K, 15kV and 1486.6eV), respectively. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out by a Perkin-Elmer TGA7 thermogravimetric analyzer under a flow of air with a temperature ramp of 10°Cmin−1 from room temperature to 800°C.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), CV, LSV and chronoamperometric measurements were all carried out on a CHI760D electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Co. Ltd., China) using a three-electrode system, which consists of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with catalyst suspensions as the working electrode (WE), a Pt wire as the counter electrode (CE), and a Ag/AgCl electrode (KCl, saturated) as the reference electrode (RE). The homogeneous catalyst suspension was prepared by dispersing 4mg of catalyst sample and 20μL 5wt% Nafion solution in 1mL mixture solvent of ethanol and deionized water (V: V=1: 4), and sonicated for 10min. 9μL catalyst suspension was uniformly loaded on the GCE surface with a micropipette, and then dried in air to form the catalyst loading about 0.51mgcm−2. The commercial Pt/C or RuO2 catalyst suspension with the same amount was loaded on the GCE surface for comparison. The EIS Nyquist plots of the samples were recorded at the potential of 1.2V in the range of 0.1 Hz–100kHz with amplitudes of 5mV in 0.2M KCl aqueous solution containing 5mMK3Fe(CN)6. The CV and LSV experiments were performed in either N2- or O2-saturated 0.1M KOH aqueous solution with a scan rate of 10mVs−1 under room temperature.

SECM measurements were performed on a CHI920C SECM (Chenhua Co. Ltd., China) with a four-electrode system. A homemade 25μm-diameter Pt-disk microelectrode was used as both the SECM tip and the work electrode 1 (WE1). The catalyst spots loaded-GCE was served as the substrate electrode and also the work electrode 2 (WE2). A Ag/AgCl electrode (KCl, saturated) and a Pt wire were used as the RE and CE, respectively. 0.1μL of each catalyst dispersion with concentration of 4mgmL−1 was dropped on the GCE surface with a micro-dispenser, then dried in air to form a catalyst array. The SECM experiments were carried out either using the redox competition mode (RC-SECM) mode for ORR or the substrate generation-tip collection (SG-TC) mode for OER in an O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution with tip-to-sample distances of ca. 50μm. In the area scan measurements, the tip was scanned in the xy-plane over the substrate GCE modified with catalyst spot array. In the SECM line scan measurements, the x- and y-line scans across the most active sites of catalyst spot arrays were performed with scan rates of 25.0μms−1. For characterizing the ORR electrocatalytic activities of catalysts, the potential applied on the SECM tip was 0.16V, the sample potentials applied on the substrate electrode (E S) were 0.56, 0.61, 0.66, 0.71 and 0.76V. For characterizing the OER catalytic performances of catalysts, the potential applied on the SECM tip was 0.16V, the sample potentials (E S) were 1.51, 1.56 and 1.61V.

The details of rotating disk electrode (RDE), rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE) and chronoamperometric measurements of the as-prepared catalysts are described in the Supporting Information. All potentials in this work were calibrated relative to a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure, composition and electric resistance of the catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the as-prepared NiCoO2/CNTs composite was characterized by SEM and TEM. The SEM image in Fig. 2 a shows that NiCoO2/CNTs exhibits a uniform cable-like 3D nanostructure with NiCoO2 nanosheets tightly sticking on the CNTs surface with the length of cylindrical nanotube about 2–5μm. The hierarchical structure can be more clearly observed in the TEM image of a single CNT combined with NiCoO2, in which the ultrathin NiCoO2 nanosheets with length of 50–70nm and thickness about 2–4nm uniformly grown on the CNT surface (Fig. 2b). The Co3O4/CNTs composite shows similar morphology and structure to those of NiCoO2/CNTs, except a slight difference in the growth density and size of the formed nanosheets (Fig. 2c,d).

The crystal structure of the NiCoO2/CNTs composite was confirmed by the XRD pattern in Fig. 2e, in which the diffraction peak at 26° belongs to the (002) plane of CNTs and the other three major diffraction peaks at 37°, 43° and 62° are assigned as the (111), (200) and (220) planes (JCPDS 10-0188) [21]. In the XRD pattern of Co3O4/CNTs (Fig. 2f), the diffraction peaks at 31°, 37°, 45°, 59° and 66° are assigned to the (220), (311), (400), (511) and (440) (JCPDS 42-1467). These XRD results confirm that NiCoO2 is face-center-cubic type and Co3O4 is spinel type [42]. The detailed information of element constitute and the chemical states of C, Co, O and Ni in NiCoO2/CNTs were further resorted by XPS. In Fig. S1a (Supporting Information), three distinct C 1s peaks at 284.5, 285.9 and 288.6eV correspond to the graphitic structure of the multiwall-CNTs (MWCNTs) [43,44], the defects on the MWCNTs surface [43,45] (regarded as the evidence of the amorphous carbon layer derived from the SPS before calcined), and the C atoms attached to the oxygen-containing moieties [43,46,47]. In Fig. S1b of the O 1s spectrum, the high energy (532.4eV) and low energy (530.4eV) peaks are in accordance with the O atoms on CNTs and in the O-Co/Ni bonding structure, respectively [48,49]. The peaks at 534.1eV can be assigned to the O atoms of the OH functional groups on the CNTs surface as well as the oxygen species in the surface-adsorbed water [43,50]. The two characteristic peaks at 781.0 and 797.2eV in the Co 2p spectrum (Fig. S1c) correspond to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 spin-orbit peaks of Co2+ [47,48], respectively. The peaks at around 786.9 and 803.9eV correspond to the shake-up type peaks of the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 edges of Co, respectively. In the Ni 2p spectrum (Fig. S1d), the typical binding energy peaks around 855.9 and 874.1eV are assigned to Ni2+ in NiO and NiOH [51]. Moreover, the Ni: Co ratio of 1:0.94 in the as-prepared composite was further determined by analysis of the XPS data. Based on the above XRD and XPS results, the successful synthesis of the Co2+-based NiCoO2/CNTs composite is confirmed.

Considering NiCoO2 and Co3O4 as the main contributors to the electrocatalytic activities of NiCoO2/CNTs and Co3O4/CNTs [22], the catalyst samples with high proportions of NiCoO2 and Co3O4 of the total weights were selected during synthesis. The loading percentages of NiCoO2/CNTs and Co3O4/CNTs were determined by TGA. Through analysis of the TGA curves in Figs. S2a and S2b (Supporting Information), the amounts of NiCoO2 and CNTs in the NiCoO2/CNTs composite were obtained as about 69.2% and 30.8%, and the amounts of Co3O4 and CNTs in the Co3O4/CNTs composite were calculated as 65.5% and 34.5%, respectively.

The electric resistance of catalyst has direct effect on the charge transfer processes of catalyst, thus further influencing its electrocatalytic activity. The EIS measurements were performed to measure and compare the electric resistances of the pure NiCoO2 (or Co3O4) and the NiCoO2/CNTs (or Co3O4/CNTs) composite. The charge-transfer resistance of the catalyst-modified electrode can be revealed by the semicircle diameter at the high frequency region in the EIS Nyquist plots. It can be seen from Figs. S3a,b (Supporting Information) that the electron transfer resistances of NiCoO2/CNTs and Co3O4/CNTs are significantly reduced compared with those of pure NiCoO2 and Co3O4, indicating that the hybrid nanostructures with CNTs as backbone can efficiently promote the charge transfer process of NiCoO2 or Co3O4 grown on their surface [52]. Besides, the ternary oxide of NiCoO2 displays a smaller semicircle diameter than that of the binary oxide of Co3O4, indicating that the improved electron-transfer performance can be achieved by composition engineering of mixed metal oxides [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activities of the catalysts for ORR
ce:section.ce:para: CV measurements were performed to assess the electrocatalytic performance of NiCoO2/CNTs towards ORR. The CV curves of other catalysts (including pure NiCoO2, pure CNTs, physically mixed NiCoO2 and CNTs (NiCoO2 +CNTs), Co3O4/CNTs and the commercial Pt/C catalyst) modified GCE and bare GCE were recorded for comparison. In Fig. 3 a, NiCoO2/CNTs shows the most positive ORR peak potential (0.78V) than those of Co3O4/CNTs (0.73V), NiCoO2 +CNTs (0.70V), pure CNTs (0.68V), bare GCE (0.61V) and pure NiCoO2 (0.60V). It indicates that through simply mixing NiCoO2 with CNTs (i.e., NiCoO2 +CNTs), the ORR peak potential shifts to more positive values (∼100mV) compared to that of pure NiCoO2, indicating the contribution of incorporated CNTs to enhance the ORR electrocatalytic activity of pure NiCoO2. It could own to the large surface area and the relatively high electric conductivity of CNTs for facilitating electron transfer step from GCE to oxygen in KOH solution [21]. After chemically growing NiCoO2 on CNTs, a significantly enhanced ORR electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs with more positive ORR peak potential compared to those of pure NiCoO2 and CNTs is obtained, which could be ascribed to the 3D structured NiCoO2/CNTs composite with nanometer-thickness of NiCoO2 sheets endowing more active sites for adsorbing oxygen and short path for the electron transfer during ORR process [18,21,53]. And the strong coupling of the NiCoO2 nanosheets with the conductive CNTs support can also enhance the electrical conductivity of NiCoO2 and further facilitate the electron transfer step during the electrocatalytic ORR process, just as proved by the EIS results. It can also be concluded from the CV results that the improved ORR electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs is from the strong coupling and synergetic effect of NiCoO2 and CNTs rather than their simply physical mixture. In addition, the ORR peak potential of NiCoO2/CNTs is also more positive than that of Co3O4/CNTs for ∼50mV, demonstrating better ORR electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2 than that of pure Co3O4. It further proves that compared to the Co-TMOs (Co3O4), the Co-MTMOs (NiCoO2) is beneficial to boost the catalytic activity of ORR, which could be contributed to the synergetic effect between Ni2+ and Co2+ with the lower activation energy for electron transfer between cations, and more donor–acceptor chemisorption sites for reversible oxygen adsorption [20,21]. Lastly, the ORR peak difference between NiCoO2/CNTs and the commercial Pt/C (0.81V) at the same mass loading is only ca. 30mV, indicating the approaching ORR electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs to Pt/C.

To gain insight of the ORR process occurred on NiCoO2/CNTs, the LSV measurements on the RDE modified with NiCoO2/CNTs were performed in an O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution. The LSV curves on the RDE modified with other catalysts were recorded for comparison. As displayed in Fig. 3b, both the onset and half-wave potentials of the ORR process of NiCoO2/CNTs (0.91, 0.70V) are more positive than those of Co3O4/CNTs (0.88, 0.65V), NiCoO2 +CNTs (0.86, 0.63V), CNTs (0.82, 0.65V) and pure NiCoO2 (0.78, 0.60V). And at the potential of 0.5V, the order of the current density magnitudes of these catalysts exhibits: NiCoO2/CNTs (4.69mAcm−2)>Co3O4/CNTs (4.63mAcm−2)>NiCoO2 +CNTs (4.57mAcm−2)>CNTs (2.97mAcm−2)>NiCoO2 (2.95mAcm−2). The most positive onset and half-wave potentials, and the highest current density of NiCoO2/CNTs compared to Co3O4/CNTs, NiCoO2 +CNTs, CNTs and NiCoO2, further prove the most efficient ORR electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs among these samples, which is in consistent with the CV results. Even the onset and half-wave potentials of NiCoO2/CNTs still have ∼90mV and ∼130mV differences compared to those of the commercial Pt/C (onset potential of 1.00V, half-wave potential of 0.83V) under the same mass loading, the low cost and the relatively good ORR electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs demonstrate its promising as a potential non-precious metal ORR electrocatalyst.

The LSV measurements with various rotation speeds at a constant scan rate were performed to understand the pathway and kinetic information of the NiCoO2/CNTs catalyzed ORR process (Fig. 3c). As shown in Fig. 3d, all the Koutecky–Levich (K-L) plots at the electrode potentials ranging from 0.35 to 0.55V obtained from the LSV curves present good linearity and near parallelism, and the slopes remain approximately constant. The average electron transfer numbers per oxygen molecule of NiCoO2/CNTs in the potential range from 0.35 to 0.55V calculated based on the K–L equations is about 3.90, demonstrating a nearly four-electron ORR pathway of NiCoO2/CNTs in 0.1M KOH [54,55], which is similar to that of the Pt/C catalyst. The catalytic pathway and the generated peroxide species (HO2 −) during the NiCoO2/CNT catalyzed ORR process were also investigated by RRDE measurements. The RRDE results displayed in Fig. S4 (Supporting Information) demonstrates the average H2O2 yield about 8.0% over the potential range of 0.20-0.65V and an electron transfer number of ∼3.84 of NiCoO2/CNTs. The low production of peroxide and approximately 4e− transfer of NiCoO2/CNTs are highly desired for an efficient ORR catalyst.

To further comparably characterize the local ORR electrocatalytic activities of these catalysts in 0.1M KOH, the SECM measurements with using RC mode [28,34–36] (or the equivalent shielding mode [56]) were performed. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the four catalyst spots with round-shape of ca. 300–400μm diameter and sample-to-sample distance about ca. 300–500μm were simultaneously loaded on the same GCE surface with the same mass loading. In the SECM measurements, different sample potentials from 0.56 to 0.76V, which were selected based on the ORR peak potentials of these catalysts in Fig. 3a, were applied on the substrate GCE. Fig. 4b-f are the obtained SECM images across the four catalyst spots and the currents displayed (Δi) are the oxygen reduction currents recorded at the SECM tip after subtracting the background currents (i.e., the tip currents across the bare GCE applied at the corresponding sample potential). In Fig. 4b, at the most negative sample potential (E S) of 0.56V, the sample spots of NiCoO2/CNTs, Co3O4/CNTs and NiCoO2 +CNTs all display bright (yellow color) images compared to the background GCE (green color), which demonstrates that NiCoO2/CNTs, Co3O4/CNTs and NiCoO2 +CNTs all show good electrocatalytic activities for ORR at this sample potential. And the NiCoO2/CNTs spot is more visible than those of Co3O4/CNTs and NiCoO2 +CNTs spots, which confirms that NiCoO2/CNTs is more active than those two towards ORR. In Fig. 4c-e, with further increasing the sample potentials to more anodic (from 0.61 to 0.71V), the color contrast of NiCoO2/CNTs compared to those of Co3O4/CNTs, NiCoO2 +CNTs and pure NiCoO2 become more pronounced, suggesting that the difference of ORR electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs compared to others become more obvious with increasing the polarized potential. It can be explained by their different ORR peak potentials in Fig. 3a. In Fig. 4f, at the sample potential of 0.76V, the ORR currents of these catalyst spots become much smaller, and even similar to the GCE background current, which could be due to consumption of oxygen by these samples at this sample potential. Furthermore, when the sample potentials become more positive (from 0.56 to 0.76V), the highest values of Δi in Fig. 4b-f decrease from 33.7 to 11.2nA, indicating that the four catalysts become less active for catalyzing ORR and the difference of local electrocatalytic activities among them become smaller with increasing the sample potentials.

To more clearly compare the differences of local ORR electrocatalytical activities of NiCoO2/CNTs to those of Co3O4/CNTs and NiCoO2 +CNT, the x-line scans across the most active sites of NiCoO2/CNTs and Co3O4/CNTs, and the y-line scans across the most active sites of NiCoO2/CNTs and NiCoO2 +CNTs were performed (shown as the dashed lines in Fig. 4b). The differences of Δi in the x- and y-line scans under different sample potentials and the extracted bar graphs are presented in Fig. 4g-j. From the figures, the Δi values of NiCoO2/CNTs are always higher than those of Co3O4/CNTs (Fig. 4g,i) and NiCoO2 +CNTs (Fig. 4h,j) at all five sample potentials. And the differences of Δi between NiCoO2/CNTs and Co3O4/CNTs, NiCoO2/CNTs and NiCoO2 +CNTs become more pronounced, further proving the better ORR electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs than those of Co3O4/CNTs and NiCoO2 +CNTs. These SECM results further prove the best ORR electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs among these catalyst samples, which are in good accordance with the CV and LSV results and additionally provide the local ORR electrocatalytic activity information of these catalysts within single SECM measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activities of the catalysts for OER
ce:section.ce:para: The OER electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs compared to other catalyst samples was evaluated in the O2-saturared 0.1M KOH solution by LSV. From the recorded LSV curve of NiCoO2/CNTs plotted in Fig. 5 a, an obvious OER electrocatalytic current is observed along with formation of small amount of oxygen bubble. In the inset of Fig. 5a, comparing with the LSV curve of Co3O4/CNTs (pink curve), the voltammetric peaks at 1.15 and 1.30V of NiCoO2/CNTs (red curve) can be contributed to the oxidation of Co2+ to Co3+ or Co4+ [3], and the enhanced peak current at the potential of 1.30V of NiCoO2/CNTs could own to the electronic coupling between Co and Ni. The onset OER potentials of these catalysts are located at 1.40, 1.48, 1.50, 1.51, 1.53, 1.58 and 1.60V for RuO2, NiCoO2/CNTs, NiCoO2 +CNTs, Co3O4/CNTs, Pt/C, NiCoO2 and CNTs, respectively. Compared to the standard reaction potential of H2O/O2 (1.23V vs. RHE) [57], NiCoO2/CNTs exhibits an overpotential (η) of only 250mV for OER, which is even still larger than that of RuO2 (170mV) but smaller compared to those of the other five catalysts. In addition, NiCoO2/CNTs can afford a current density of 10mAcm−2 (denoted as η J10, a characteristic value to the benchmark OER catalyst [12,14,58,59]) at a small overpotential of 0.42V, which is lower than those of NiCoO2 +CNTs (0.46V), Co3O4/CNTs (0.47V), Pt/C (0.53V) and significantly lower than those of NiCoO2 and CNTs. The earlier onset potential and smaller η J10 of NiCoO2/CNTs indicate its higher OER electrocatalytic activity than NiCoO2 +CNTs, Co3O4/CNTs, NiCoO2, CNTs and Pt/C. It could be ascribed to that the CNTs support with high electrical conductivity could facilitate the charge transfer during OER at the NiCoO2/CNTs–electrolyte interface [4], the nanosheet-structured NiCoO2 could provide more efficient active sites for catalyzing OER, and Ni2+ provides active sites for OER with lower activation potential compared with Co2+ [5]. The Tafel plots of these catalysts at the potential range of 1.55 to 1.76V were calculated to understand their electrocatalytic kinetics for OER. The resulting Tafel slopes of these catalysts in Fig. 5b show: RuO2 (119mVdec−1)<NiCoO2/CNTs (156mVdec−1)<NiCoO2 +CNTs (167mVdec−1)<Co3O4/CNTs (171mVdec−1)<Pt/C (180mVdec−1)<NiCoO2 (199mVdec−1), which corresponds to their OER electrocatalytic activity performance as RuO2 >NiCoO2/CNTs>NiCoO2 +CNTs>Co3O4/CNTs>Pt/C>NiCoO2. The relatively small Tafel slope of NiCoO2/CNTs enables it to afford large current densities at low overpotentials [10,11], suggesting its favorable kinetics for OER. And even though the OER electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs is still inferior to that of the golden standard OER catalyst (RuO2), the merits of low cost and efficient OER electrocatalytic activity makes NiCoO2/CNTs as a promising alternative to the precious metal-based OER catalyst.

SECM with SG-TC mode [37–39] was employed to comparably investigate the local OER electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs with other catalysts in 0.1M KOH solution. The GCE modified with catalyst samples (Fig. 6 a) were polarized at three sample potentials of 1.51, 1.56 and 1.61V, at which potentials the catalyst samples could produce sufficient oxygen for SECM tip to collect and the OER electrocatalytic activities of these catalysts can be well distinguished (Fig. 5a). The SECM tip, with applying a potential of 0.16V to completely reduce the generated oxygen from the catalyst spots on the GCE surface, was scanned across the catalyst spot surfaces. Fig. 6b-d are the obtained SECM images of the four catalyst samples at these three sample potentials after subtracting the background currents (i.e., the oxygen reduction currents at the tip over the bare GCE with applying corresponding sample potentials). It can be seen from the figures that NiCoO2/CNTs always exhibits brighter color than those of Co3O4/CNTs at all sample potentials, indicating a higher OER catalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs than that of Co3O4/CNTs. In Fig. 6b,c, at the sample potentials (E S) of 1.51 and 1.56V, the magnitudes of the ORR currents of NiCoO2 +CNTs and pure NiCoO2 are larger than those of NiCoO2/CNTs, suggesting a relatively higher OER catalytic activities of NiCoO2 +CNTs and pure NiCoO2 than that of NiCoO2/CNTs at these two sample potentials. It could be due to that the close current densities of NiCoO2/CNTs and NiCoO2 +CNTs at these two sample potentials (as shown in Fig. 5a), and the produced oxygen from NiCoO2 +CNTs could diffuse to the adjacent NiCoO2 sample during SECM scanning, which leads to a high ORR current detected for the pure NiCoO2. In Fig. 6d, when E S moves to the more cathodic value of 1.61V, NiCoO2/CNTs shows the brightest image among these four catalyst spots, suggesting its best OER electrocatalytic performance among these samples. In addition, it can also be observed the “cloudy” images around the sample spots in the SECM images, which could be from the evolved oxygen during SECM scanning. It also demonstrates the capability of SECM in in situ “observing” the oxygen evolution process occurred on the catalyst surface.

The difference of local OER electrocatalytical activities of NiCoO2/CNTs compared with those of Co3O4/CNTs and NiCoO2 +CNT at different sample potentials are better presented through SECM line scan measurements. The x-line scans across the most active sites of NiCoO2/CNTs and Co3O4/CNTs, and the y-line scans across the most active sites of NiCoO2/CNTs and NiCoO2 +CNTs were carried out (shown as dashed lines in Fig. 6b). The obtained line scan results and the extracted bar graphs are displayed in Figs. 6e–h. In Fig. 6e,g, the Δi values of NiCoO2/CNTs are always larger than those of Co3O4/CNTs at these three sample potentials, indicating its higher OER electrocatalytic activity than that of Co3O4/CNTs. In Figs. 6f,h, the Δi values of NiCoO2/CNTs are comparable with those of NiCoO2 +CNTs at the potentials of 1.51 and 1.56V. After increasing the sample potential to 1.61V, the value of Δi of NiCoO2/CNTs is much larger than that of NiCoO2 +CNTs, which proves the better OER electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs than that of NiCoO2/CNTs. With increasing E S from 1.51 to 1.61V, the Δi values of the three samples all present rising trends, which could be due to that more oxygen can be produced from the catalyst sample at more positive potentials. Moreover, the differences of Δi between NiCoO2/CNTs and Co3O4/CNTs, and NiCoO2/CNTs and NiCoO2 +CNTs, become more obvious with increasing E S. The above SECM area and line scan results further confirm the best electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs among these four catalysts toward OER, which is consistent with the LSV result. And through applying different potentials on catalyst samples, the quantitative comparison of the local OER electrocatalytic activities of samples was successfully obtained by SECM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bifunctional oxygen electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs
ce:section.ce:para:
To assess the overall bifunctional electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs for both ORR and OER, the difference between the oxygen evolution potential at the current density of 10mAcm−2 and the oxygen reduction potential at the current density of −3mAcm−2 (i.e., ΔE = EJ
10,OER−EJ
‑3,ORR) was calculated. As illustrated in Table S1 (Supporting Information), the ΔE value of NiCoO2/CNTs was deduced to be 0.99V, which is competitive with the value of Pt/C (ΔE Pt/C =0.93V) obtained in this work and much smaller than the value of RuO2 (ΔE RuO2 =1.39V) reported in the literature [60]. It reassures the comparable bifunctional electrocatalytic activity of NiCoO2/CNTs to Pt/C and better activity than that of RuO2. And compared to Co3O4/CNTs, NiCoO2/CNTs has more positive onset potential for ORR, smaller OER onset potential and ΔE, indicating its higher electrocatalytic activities toward both ORR and OER than those of Co3O4/CNTs. Further comparing with the previously reported other Ni-Co3+ MTMOs composites, NiCoO2/CNTs also exhibits comparable or better bifunctional oxygen catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methanol tolerance and electrochemical stability of NiCoO2/CNTs
ce:section.ce:para: The tolerance to methanol and electrochemical stability are the other two major challenges influencing the performance of difunctional oxygen catalysts and their practical applications. The methanol tolerance of NiCoO2/CNTs compared with the commercial Pt/C catalyst were tested through CV and chronoamperometric measurements in the O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution after adding 3M methanol. Fig. 7 a shows the CV curves of NiCoO2/CNTs before and after addition of methanol, in which the ORR peaks at 0.78V is clearly observed in the O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution and no conspicuous change occurred after injecting 3M methanol, suggesting the excellent tolerance of NiCoO2/CNTs to methanol. For the commercial Pt/C catalyst, with adding 3M methanol into solution, the ORR peak disappears whereas a typical oxidation peak of methanol at 0.90V appears (Fig. 7b), indicating its poor methanol tolerance [61,62]. The chronoamperometric curve of NiCoO2/CNTs at a constant oxygen reduction potential of 0.76V in Fig. 7c also proves its better methanol tolerance ability than that of Pt/C. At the beginning 0–300s without adding methanol, both NiCoO2/CNTs and Pt/C exhibit ORR currents at the potential of 0.76V as expected. After adding methanol into the solution at around 300s, the two samples exhibit totally different amperometric responses: nearly no change of current at 0.76V is observed for NiCoO2/CNTs from 300–1000s, while the current sharply jumps from negative to positive value at around 300s for Pt/C due to the methanol oxidation reaction. Both the CV and chronoamperometric results prove that NiCoO2/CNTs has better ability to avoid the crossover effect from methanol relative to the Pt/C catalyst.

The electrochemical stability of NiCoO2/CNTs compared with Pt/C in the O2-saturated 0.1M KOH was estimated by running continuous chronoamperometry at the ORR potential of 0.76V over 10000s. As shown in Fig. 7d, the recorded current-time curves for both NiCoO2/CNTs and Pt/C exhibit gradual decrease trend with time. For NiCoO2/CNTs, the current-time curve shows relatively slow attenuation with the ORR current remaining 98.5% of the initial current at 10000s, indicating the good stability of NiCoO2/CNTs in alkaline media. It could own to the ultrathin nanosheet structure of NiCoO2/CNTs and the CNTs support avoid the possible aggregation of nanostructured NiCoO2 over time, thus maintaining the catalytic active sites of NiCoO2 nanostructures for ORR. While the Pt/C catalyst presents low electrochemical stability for ORR with the ORR current remaining 91.9% of the initial current at 10000s. The higher insensitivity to methanol and the better stability of NiCoO2/CNTs in comparison with the commercial Pt/C catalyst highlight the advantage of NiCoO2/CNTs as the ORR electrocatalyst than the commercial Pt-based catalysts.

The long-term OER chronopotentiometric stability test of NiCoO2/CNTs at a constant current density of 10mAcm−2 with a rotation speed of 900rpm was performed in the O2-saturated 0.1M KOH electrolyte. The electrochemical stabilities of the Pt/C and RuO2 catalysts were comparably tested. In the recorded chronopotentiometric curves in Fig. 7e, it can be observed that NiCoO2/CNTs exhibits an excellent stability with only a slight potential increase of 40mV after 12h, which is similar to the stability of the commercial RuO2 catalyst within the same time period. It could be contributed to the 3D hierarchical nanosheet-structure of NiCoO2/CNTs providing more storage space for the generated oxygen bubbles and avoiding the oxygen bubbles blocking the catalytic sites. The combination with the CNTs support could also prevent the structure collapse of the NiCoO2 nanosheets during long the electrocatalytic process. Whereas, the Pt/C catalyst displays a sharp potential increase of 150mV within 3h, indicating the poor stability of Pt/C for OER in alkaline media [3]. The experimental result of stability highlights the comparable stability of NiCoO2/CNTs to the commercial RuO2 catalyst and its apparent advantage over Pt/C catalyst for electrocatalyzing OER. Thus, based on the desirable electrocatalytic activities and good electrochemical stability for both ORR and OER, NiCoO2/CNTs produced in this work shows promising as a novel non-precious metal oxygen catalyst to replace the Pt/C catalyst with the weak OER catalytic performance and the expensive RuO2 catalyst.

In this work, a 3D nanosheet-structured NiCoO2/CNTs hybrid composite was successfully synthesized using a facile one-pot method. The better ORR and OER electrocatalytic activities of NiCoO2/CNTs than those of the pure NiCoO2, pure CNTs, the physically mixed NiCoO2 and CNTs in alkaline medium were proved by CV and LSV measurements. The SECM analysis also proves the better local ORR and OER electrocatalytic activities of NiCoO2/CNTs compared to the other catalyst samples, and also “visualized” the electrocatalytic oxygen evolution process on these catalyst surfaces. The closed oxygen reduction peak and half-wave potentials, and the nearly four-electron dominated ORR process obtained by the Koutecky–Levich plots confirm the comparable ORR electrocatalytic performance of NiCoO2/CNTs to the commercial Pt/C catalyst. The experimental results of methanol tolerance and electrochemical stability prove the better performance of NiCoO2/CNTs than Pt/C catalyst for ORR, and the similar stability to that of the RuO2 catalyst for OER. This work reports the novel Co2+-based mixed transition metal oxides composite as the bifunctional electrocatalyst for both ORR and OER for the first time. And it raises the prospect of NiCoO2/CNTs as a new low-cost, non-precious metal bifunctional catalyst in reversible fuel cells, metal-air batteries and other sustainable energy technologies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21105079, 51273158, 21405119), the Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars by the State Education Ministry of China, the International Science and Technology Cooperation and Exchange Program of Shaanxi Province of China (2016KW-064), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China, the General Financial Grant from the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M592773), the Chinese Government Scholarship (201506285022) and the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (KAKENHI: 16H06042).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2017.08.192.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Characterisation of bifunctional electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction and evolution by means of SECM
Characterization of local electrocatalytical activity of nanosheet-structured ZnCo2O4/carbon nanotubes composite for oxygen reduction reaction with scanning electrochemical microscopy

Graphene anchored with Co3O4 nanoparticles as anode of lithium ion batteries with enhanced reversible capacity and cyclic performance
Chemical oxidation of multiwalled carbon nanotubes
Work functions and surface functional groups of multiwall carbon nanotubes
Functionalization of carbon nanotubes by potassium permanganate assisted with phase transfer catalyst
Lithium insertion into multi-walled raw carbon nanotubes pre-doped with lithium
High resolution XPS study of a thin CoO (111) film grown on Co(0001)

Reduced graphene oxide/nickel cobaltite nanoflake composites for high specific capacitance supercapacitors

Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
Direct observation of oxygen depletion and product formation during photocatalysis at a TiO2 surface using scanning electrochemical microscopy
A reliable aerosol-spray-assisted approach to produce and optimize amorphous metal oxide catalysts for electrochemical water splitting
In situ electrochemical oxidation tuning of transition metal disulfides to oxides for enhanced water oxidation
Metal–organic framework derived hybrid Co3O4-carbon porous nanowire arrays as reversible oxygen evolution electrodes
Nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped graphene/carbon nanosheets as bifunctional electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction and evolution
Mesoporous N-doped carbons prepared with thermally removable nanoparticle templates: an efficient electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction
